                  ydiff = md("$$Y_t(u) - Y_c(u)$$")) |>
    cols_move(columns = c(ytreat, ycontrol), after = c(subject)) |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(align = "left", v_align = "middle", size = "large"), 
              locations = cells_column_labels(columns = c(subject))) |>
    cols_align(align = "center", columns = everything()) |>
    cols_align(align = "left", columns = c(subject)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Outcomes", c(ytreat, ycontrol)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Causal Effect", c(ydiff)) |>
    fmt_markdown(columns = everything())|> 
  tab_style(style = cell_text(v_align = "bottom"),
            locations = cells_column_labels())|> 
  tab_style(style = cell_text(v_align = "bottom"),
            locations = cells_column_labels())
```

If we make the assumption that there is a single value for $\tau$ *and* that $1$ is a good estimate of that value, then we can determine the missing potential outcomes. The Preceptor Table no longer has any missing values, so we can use it to easily answer (almost) any conceivable question.

### Two values for tau

A second model might assume that the causal effect is different between levels of a category but the same within those levels. For example, perhaps there is a $\tau_F$ for females and $\tau_M$ for males where $\tau_F$ might not equal $\tau_M$. We are making this assumption to give us a different model with which we can fill in the missing values in our Preceptor Table. We can't make any progress unless we make some assumptions. That is an inescapable consequence of the *Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference*.

A covariate is any variable outside of the (potential) outcomes. In this case, possible covariates include, but are not limited to, sex, age, political party and almost everything else which might be associated with attitudes toward immigration. Consider a model in which causal effects differ based on sex. (Don't forget that "causal effect" means, by convention, the same thing as *average* causal effect when we are discussing the results of a data science project.) 

<!-- Not sure that I like this table. Also, maybe introduce the notion of covariates in a predictive context first. Knowing sex helps to predict Yao's attitude toward immigration, regardless of causal effects. -->

```{r}
# First, we create a tibble with the values we want for the table

tibble(subject = c("Yao", "Emma", "Cassidy", "Tahmid", "Diego"),
       Sex = c("Male", "Female", "Female", "Male", "Male"),
       ytreat = c("13", "14", "$$6 + \\tau_F$$", "$$12 + \\tau_M$$", "3"),
       ycontrol = c("$$13 - \\tau_M$$", "$$14 - \\tau_F$$", "6", "12", "$$3 - \\tau_M$$"),
       ydiff = c("$$\\tau_M$$", "$$\\tau_F$$", "$$\\tau_F$$", "$$\\tau_M$$", "$$\\tau_M$$")) |>
  
  # Then, we use the gt function to make it pretty
  
  gt() |>
    tab_header(title = "Preceptor Table") |> 
    cols_label(subject = md("ID"),
                  ytreat = md("$$Y_t(u)$$"),
                  ycontrol = md("$$Y_c(u)$$"),
                  ydiff = md("$$Y_t(u) - Y_c(u)$$")) |>
    cols_move(columns = c(ytreat, ycontrol), after = c(subject)) |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(align = "left", v_align = "middle", size = "large"), 
              locations = cells_column_labels(columns = c(subject))) |>
    cols_align(align = "center", columns = everything()) |>
    cols_align(align = "left", columns = c(subject)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Outcomes", c(ytreat, ycontrol)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Causal Effect", c(ydiff)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Covariate", c(Sex)) |>
    fmt_markdown(columns = everything())|> 
  tab_style(style = cell_text(v_align = "bottom"),
            locations = cells_column_labels())
```

Our estimate for $\tau_M$ would be $$(13+3)/2 - 12 = -4$$ while that for $\tau_F$ would be $$14-6 = +8$$.

Using those values, we would fill out our new table like this:

```{r}
# First, we create a tibble with the values we want for the table

tibble(subject = c("Yao", "Emma", "Cassidy", "Tahmid", "Diego"),
       ytreat = c("13", "14", "$$6 + (+8)$$", "$$12 + (-4)$$", "3"),
       ycontrol = c("$$13 - (-4)$$", "$$14 - (+8)$$", "6", "12", "$$3 - (-4)$$"),
       ydiff = c("-4", "+8", "+8", "-4", "-4")) |>
  
  # Then, we use the gt function to make it pretty
  
  gt() |>
    tab_header(title = "Preceptor Table") |> 
    cols_label(subject = md("ID"),
                  ytreat = md("$$Y_t(u)$$"),
                  ycontrol = md("$$Y_c(u)$$"),
                  ydiff = md("$$Y_t(u) - Y_c(u)$$")) |>
    cols_move(columns = c(ytreat, ycontrol), after = c(subject)) |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(align = "left", v_align = "middle", size = "large"), 
              locations = cells_column_labels(columns = c(subject))) |>
    cols_align(align = "center", columns = everything()) |>
    cols_align(align = "left", columns = c(subject)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Outcomes", c(ytreat, ycontrol)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Causal Effect", c(ydiff)) |>
    fmt_markdown(columns = everything())|> 
  tab_style(style = cell_text(v_align = "bottom"),
            locations = cells_column_labels())
```

Which gives us:

<!-- Y_c for Yao can't be 17! Biggest value is 15. Change it or discuss it. -->

```{r}
# First, we create a tibble with the values we want for the table

tibble(subject = c("Yao", "Emma", "Cassidy", "Tahmid", "Diego"),
       ytreat = c("13", "14", "14", "8", "3"),
       ycontrol = c("17", "6", "6", "12", "7"),
       ydiff = c("-4", "+8", "+8", "-4", "-4")) |>
  
  # Then, we use the gt function to make it pretty
  
  gt() |>
    tab_header(title = "Preceptor Table") |> 
    cols_label(subject = md("ID"),
                  ytreat = md("$$Y_t(u)$$"),
                  ycontrol = md("$$Y_c(u)$$"),
                  ydiff = md("$$Y_t(u) - Y_c(u)$$")) |>
    cols_move(columns = c(ytreat, ycontrol), after = c(subject)) |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(align = "left", v_align = "middle", size = "large"), 
              locations = cells_column_labels(columns = c(subject))) |>
    cols_align(align = "center", columns = everything()) |>
    cols_align(align = "left", columns = c(subject)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Outcomes", c(ytreat, ycontrol)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Causal Effect", c(ydiff)) |>
    fmt_markdown(columns = everything())|> 
  tab_style(style = cell_text(v_align = "bottom"),
            locations = cells_column_labels())
```

We now have two different estimates for Emma (and for everyone else in the table). When we estimate $Y_c(Emma)$ using an assumption of constant treatment effect (a single value for $\tau$), we get $Y_c(Emma) = 13$. When we estimate assuming treatment effect is constant for each sex, we calculate that $Y_c(Emma) = 8$. This difference between our estimates for Emma highlights the difficulties of inference. Models drive inference. Different models will produce different inferences.

### Heterogenous treatment effects

Is the assumption of a constant treatment effect, $\tau$, usually true? No! It is never true. People vary. The effect of a pill on you will always be different from the effect of a pill on your friend, at least if we measure outcomes accurately enough. Treatment effects are always *heterogeneous*, meaning that they vary across individuals.

Reality looks like this:

```{r}
# First, we create a tibble with the values we want for the table

tibble(subject = c("Yao", "Emma", "Cassidy", "Tahmid", "Diego"),
       ytreat = c("13", "14", "$$6 + \\tau_{cassidy}$$", "$$12 + \\tau_{tahmid}$$", "3"),
       ycontrol = c("$$13 - \\tau_{yao}$$", "$$14 - \\tau_{emma}$$", "6", "12", "$$3 - \\tau_{diego}$$"),
       ydiff = c("$$\\tau_{yao}$$", "$$\\tau_{emma}$$", "$$\\tau_{cassidy}$$", "$$\\tau_{tahmid}$$", "$$\\tau_{diego}$$")) |>
  
  # Then, we use the gt function to make it pretty
  
  gt() |>
    tab_header(title = "Preceptor Table") |> 
    cols_label(subject = md("ID"),
                  ytreat = md("$$Y_t(u)$$"),
                  ycontrol = md("$$Y_c(u)$$"),
                  ydiff = md("$$Y_t(u) - Y_c(u)$$")) |>
    cols_move(columns = c(ytreat, ycontrol), after = c(subject)) |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(align = "left", v_align = "middle", size = "large"), 
              locations = cells_column_labels(columns = c(subject))) |>
    cols_align(align = "center", columns = everything()) |>
    cols_align(align = "left", columns = c(subject)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Outcomes", c(ytreat, ycontrol)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Causal Effect", c(ydiff)) |>
    fmt_markdown(columns = everything())|> 
  tab_style(style = cell_text(v_align = "bottom"),
            locations = cells_column_labels())
```

Can we solve for $\tau_{yao}$? No! That is the *Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference*. Instead, we usually focus on the average causal effect for the entire population.

<!-- DK: Awkward to discuss ATE above before introducing it here. -->

### Average treatment effect

The average treatment effect (ATE), as discussed above, is the **average** difference in *potential* outcomes between the treated group and the control groups. Because averaging is a linear operator, the average difference is the same as the difference between the averages. The distinction between this estimand and estimands like $\tau$, $\tau_M$ and $\tau_F$, is that, in this case, we do not care about using the average treatment effect to fill in missing values in each row. The average treatment effect is useful because we don't have to assume anything about each individuals' $\tau$, like $\tau_{yao}$, but can still understand something about the average causal effect across the whole population.

As we did before, the simplest way to estimate the ATE is to take the mean of the treated group ($10$) and the mean of the control group ($9$) and then take the difference in those means ($1$). If we use this method to an estimate of the ATE, we'll call it $\widehat{ATE}$, pronounced "ATE-hat."

As we noted before, this is a popular estimand. Why?

1.  There's an obvious *estimator* for this estimand: the mean difference of the *observed* outcomes between the treated group and the control group: $\overline{Y_t(u)} - \overline{Y_c(u)}$.

2.  If treatment is *randomly assigned*, the estimator is *unbiased*: you can be fairly confident in the estimate if you have a large enough treatment and control groups.

3.  If you are willing to assume that the causal effect is the same for everyone (a big assumption!), you can use your estimate of the ATE, $\widehat{ATE}$, to fill in the missing individual values in your Preceptor Table.

Just because the ATE is often a useful estimand doesn't mean that it *always* is.

Consider point #3. For example, let's say the treatment effect does vary dependent on sex. For males there is a small negative effect (-4), but for females there is a larger positive effect (+8). However, the average treatment effect for the whole sample, even if you estimate it correctly, will be a single positive number (+1) -- since the positive effect for females is larger than the negative effect for males.

<!-- DK: Awkward paragraph. -->

Estimating the average treatment effect, by calculating $\widehat{ATE}$, is easy. But is our $\widehat{ATE}$ a good estimate of the actual ATE? After all, if we knew all the missing values in the Preceptor Table, we could calculate the ATE perfectly. But those missing values may be wildly different from the observed values. Consider this Preceptor Table:

```{r}
# First, we create a tibble with the values we want for the table

tibble(subject = c("Yao", "Emma", "Cassidy", "Tahmid", "Diego"),
       ytreat = c("13", "14", "9", "15", "3"),
       ycontrol = c("10", "11", "6", "12", "0"),
       ydiff = c("+3", "+3", "+3", "+3", "+3")) |>
  
  # Then, we use the gt function to make it pretty
  
  gt() |>
    tab_header(title = "Preceptor Table") |> 
    cols_label(subject = md("ID"),
                  ytreat = md("$$Y_t(u)$$"),
                  ycontrol = md("$$Y_c(u)$$"),
                  ydiff = md("$$Y_t(u) - Y_c(u)$$")) |>
    cols_move(columns = c(ytreat, ycontrol), after = c(subject)) |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(align = "left", v_align = "middle", size = "large"), 
              locations = cells_column_labels(columns = c(subject))) |>
    cols_align(align = "center", columns = everything()) |>
    cols_align(align = "left", columns = c(subject)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Outcomes", c(ytreat, ycontrol)) |>
    tab_spanner(label = "Causal Effect", c(ydiff)) |>
    fmt_markdown(columns = everything())|> 
  tab_style(style = cell_text(v_align = "bottom"),
            locations = cells_column_labels())
```

In this example, there is indeed a constant treatment effect for everyone: $+3$. Note that the *observed* values are all the same, but the unobserved values were such that our estimated ATE, $+1$, is pretty far from the actual ATE, $+3$. If we think we have a reasonable estimate of ATE, using that value as a constant for $\tau$ might be our *best guess*.

## A worked example: the financial-counseling program

The previous four sections built the Rubin Causal Model from the ground up --- one new piece at a time. This section ties them together. We pick a single concrete scenario, draw both Preceptor Tables and both Population Tables for it, and walk through the four assumptions as they apply to a realistic problem. The matching tutorial in the **[primer.tutorials](https://ppbds.github.io/primer/tutorials/)** package (`rubin-causal-model`) asks the same conceptual questions in `learnr` exercise form; this section gives the underlying reasoning the tutorial does not have room for.

Imagine that you are a data scientist at a mid-size technology company. The Head of HR has a big-picture goal --- improve employees' long-term financial wellbeing --- and many decisions to make in service of it: which benefits to offer, which to cut, how aggressively to nudge employees toward higher retirement-account contributions, and whether to fund a new one-on-one financial-counseling program where employees can meet with a certified financial planner. The HR Director trusts you to find the evidence that helps her decide. This section walks through the four Cardinal Virtues by applying them to two related questions about the same employees: a causal one (what is the effect of the financial-counseling program on 401(k) contribution rates?) and a paired predictive one (what will be the average 401(k) contribution rate among next year's new hires?). There are many decisions to make.

A bit of real-world context before we begin. The U.S. private-sector 401(k) system covers a substantial fraction of working-age Americans, but participation rates --- the share of eligible employees who actually contribute --- vary enormously across employers, and the single biggest driver of that variation is whether the plan defaults employees in or requires them to opt in. The behavioral-economics literature, most famously Madrian and Shea's 2001 *Quarterly Journal of Economics* paper, showed that a single corporate switch from opt-in to opt-out default enrollment lifted participation from about 37% to about 86%. That finding was so robust it eventually drove federal policy: the SECURE Act 2.0, signed in December 2022, now requires most newly established 401(k) plans to automatically enroll eligible employees at a starting contribution rate of at least 3%. Beyond automatic enrollment, large employers have increasingly added *financial-wellness benefits* over the last decade: one-on-one sessions with certified financial planners, financial-literacy webinars, debt-management counseling, and so on. The HR Director's question --- whether the new counseling program would meaningfully shift contribution rates --- sits inside this broader landscape. Voluntary financial-counseling programs raise a chronic concern, which the chapter's earlier section on unconfoundedness already hinted at: the employees who sign up for such programs tend to be those already more financially attentive than average. Sorting out the causal effect of the program from the self-selection of its participants is the central methodological problem.

### Wisdom

> *Wisdom begins with a question and then moves on to the creation of a Preceptor Table and an examination of our data.*

Wisdom commits us to a question that is precise enough to be answered. The Preceptor Table makes the commitment concrete: it is the smallest table that, if every cell were filled with the truth, would make the question's answer easy to read off.

This is the same Tukey wisdom from earlier in the chapter. *The combination of some data and an aching desire for an answer does not ensure that a reasonable answer can be extracted from a given body of data.* If we cannot describe the table that would answer our question, no amount of cleverness in modeling will save us.

#### The primary question (causal)

The primary question is:

> What is the average causal effect of the financial-counseling program on an employee's 401(k) contribution rate?

This is a causal question. Each employee has two potential outcomes --- the 401(k) contribution rate they would have under the counseling program, and the rate they would have without it --- and the causal effect for that employee is the difference. The average causal effect across all employees is the number the HR Director wants to weigh against the program's cost. The Preceptor Table that would let us read this off has four columns: the employee identifier, two potential-outcome columns (one per treatment value), and one treatment column naming which arm the employee actually ended up in.

The treatment values are *Counseled* (the employee attended at least one one-on-one session with a certified financial planner during the calendar year) and *Not Counseled* (the employee did not). The outcome is the employee's annual 401(k) contribution as a percent of salary --- a continuous variable, in practice clustered between 0% and 15% for active participants.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
rcm_pt_primary_tibble <- tibble::tribble(
  ~`Employee`,         ~`Contribution if Counseled`, ~`Contribution if Not Counseled`, ~`Counseling Status`,
  "Alice Becker",      "8.0",                        "5.0",                            "Counseled",
  "Brendan Cohen",     "10.0",                       "10.0",                           "Not Counseled",
  "...",               "...",                        "...",                            "...",
  "Diego Ramirez",     "12.5",                       "9.5",                            "Counseled"
)

rcm_pt_primary_html <- gt::gt(rcm_pt_primary_tibble, id = "rcm_pt_primary") |>
  gt::tab_header(title = "Preceptor Table --- Primary (causal) question") |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Unit",                id = "unit_span",      columns = c(`Employee`)) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Potential Outcomes", id = "outcome_span",   columns = c(`Contribution if Counseled`, `Contribution if Not Counseled`)) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Treatment",          id = "treatment_span", columns = c(`Counseling Status`)) |>
  gt::cols_align(align = "left",  columns = c(`Employee`, `Counseling Status`)) |>
  gt::cols_align(align = "right", columns = c(`Contribution if Counseled`, `Contribution if Not Counseled`)) |>
  gt::fmt_markdown(columns = gt::everything()) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    heading.title.font.size   = "1.5em",
    heading.title.font.weight = "bolder",
    column_labels.font.weight = "normal"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners()) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(align = "left"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = c("unit_span", "treatment_span"))) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(align = "right"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = "outcome_span")) |>
  gt::text_transform(
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = `Contribution if Not Counseled`, rows = c(1, 4)),
    fn = function(x) hatch(x)
  ) |>
  gt::text_transform(
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = `Contribution if Counseled`, rows = 2),
    fn = function(x) hatch(x)
  ) |>
  gt::tab_footnote(
    footnote = "If all the information in this table were available, we could answer the question: What is the average causal effect of the financial-counseling program on an employee's 401(k) contribution rate?",
    locations = gt::cells_title()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Each row is one of the company's roughly 2,400 benefits-eligible employees. The cross-hatched cell in each row marks the unobservable counterfactual --- the contribution rate the employee would have had under the treatment they did not receive.",
    locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = "outcome_span")
  ) |>
  gt::tab_footnote(
    footnote = "The treatment is whether the employee attended at least one one-on-one session with a certified financial planner during the calendar year.",
    locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = "treatment_span")
  ) |>
  gt::opt_css(css = "#rcm_pt_primary .gt_footnote { max-width: 1px; word-break: break-word; }") |>
  gt::as_raw_html()

cat("```{=html}\n",
    '<div style="display: inline-block; width: auto; max-width: 100%;">',
    rcm_pt_primary_html, "</div>\n```\n", sep = "")
```

Look closely at how the table marks the unobservable cells. Alice was *Counseled* (her actual treatment), so her `Contribution if Counseled` value (8.0%) is observable in principle. Her `Contribution if Not Counseled` value (5.0%) is cross-hatched: it is the truth in some metaphysical sense --- there is a contribution rate she *would have set* under the counterfactual where the company did not offer counseling --- but no empirical process could ever recover it. The whole machinery of the Rubin Causal Model exists to estimate the cross-hatched cells without ever observing them. Cleverness in modeling, in the end, is cleverness about how to fill in cells that can never be measured.

A subtle point worth dwelling on: Brendan Cohen's row shows the *same* contribution value under both treatments (10.0%). That's not a coding error; it is a genuine possibility in the Rubin framework. Some employees would contribute the same regardless of whether the company offered counseling --- their behavior is unaffected by the treatment. For those employees the unit-level causal effect is exactly zero. Other employees would respond differently. The average causal effect across the population is the average of all these unit-level effects, ranging from zero to whatever the largest individual effect is.

#### The paired question (predictive)

The paired question is:

> What will be the average 401(k) contribution rate of new hires next year?

This is a predictive question. We want one specific number: an estimate of the average contribution rate for the next cohort of new hires (the company adds about 300 net new hires annually), with its associated uncertainty. The Preceptor Table that would answer it has only two columns: an employee identifier and a single outcome column. No potential outcomes; no treatment.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
rcm_pt_paired_tibble <- tibble::tribble(
  ~`New Hire`,           ~`Contribution Rate (%)`,
  "Erica Foster",        "6.0",
  "Frank Gutierrez",     "10.0",
  "...",                 "...",
  "Hannah Park",         "4.5"
)

rcm_pt_paired_html <- gt::gt(rcm_pt_paired_tibble, id = "rcm_pt_paired") |>
  gt::tab_header(title = "Preceptor Table --- Paired (predictive) question") |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Unit",    id = "unit_span",    columns = c(`New Hire`)) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Outcome", id = "outcome_span", columns = c(`Contribution Rate (%)`)) |>
  gt::cols_align(align = "left",  columns = c(`New Hire`)) |>
  gt::cols_align(align = "right", columns = c(`Contribution Rate (%)`)) |>
  gt::fmt_markdown(columns = gt::everything()) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    heading.title.font.size   = "1.5em",
    heading.title.font.weight = "bolder",
    column_labels.font.weight = "normal"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners()) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(align = "left"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = "unit_span")) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(align = "right"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = "outcome_span")) |>
  gt::tab_footnote(
    footnote = "If all the information in this table were available, we could answer the question: What will be the average 401(k) contribution rate of new hires next year?",
    locations = gt::cells_title()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Each row is one new hire expected next year. The company adds about 300 net new hires annually.",
    locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = "unit_span")
  ) |>
  gt::tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Annual 401(k) contribution as a percent of salary.",
    locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = "outcome_span")
  ) |>
  gt::opt_css(css = "#rcm_pt_paired .gt_footnote { max-width: 1px; word-break: break-word; }") |>
  gt::as_raw_html()

cat("```{=html}\n",
    '<div style="display: inline-block; width: auto; max-width: 100%;">',
    rcm_pt_paired_html, "</div>\n```\n", sep = "")
```

#### How the two tables differ

The two tables are nearly identical in structure. Same kind of unit. Same kind of outcome. The structural differences are exactly two: the causal table has *two* potential-outcome columns (with the unobservable counterfactual hatched) instead of one, and it has a Treatment column under its own spanner. Everything else is the same. That single difference in outcome-and-treatment bookkeeping is the entire mechanical difference between predictive and causal models. The same dataset --- and, in later chapters, the same R code --- can fit either question. What makes one *causal* and the other *predictive* is the analyst's commitment about whether a covariate counts as a treatment.

### Justice

> *Justice reviews the Population Table and selects the formula for the data generating mechanism.*

Justice is the section of the analysis where you (or your critics) raise concerns about whether your model will work. The Population Table is where those concerns become concrete: it bridges between the data we actually have and the Preceptor Table we wish we had.

The four Justice concerns are *validity*, *stability*, *representativeness*, and *unconfoundedness*. The first three apply to both predictive and causal models. The fourth applies only to causal models --- which means it is central to the primary question in this section but irrelevant to the paired predictive question.

#### The Population Table for the primary causal question

For our setting, the data we have is from a comparable tech company that ran a similar financial-counseling program in 2022. The Preceptor Table is our own 2026 employees. The Population Table covers both, plus the much larger universe of technology-company employees in any year between 2022 and 2026. Two notations mark unobservable potential outcomes: in Data rows, the unobserved counterfactual is `...` (we did not measure it); in Preceptor rows, the unobservable counterfactual is hatched (it is the truth, but no empirical process could ever reveal it).

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
rcm_pop_primary_tibble <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Source,     ~`Employee`,         ~`Year`, ~`Contribution if Counseled`, ~`Contribution if Not Counseled`, ~`Counseling Status`,
  "...",       "...",               "...",   "...",                        "...",                            "...",
  "Data",      "Sarah Kim",         "2022",  "9.0",                        "...",                            "Counseled",
  "Data",      "Michael Lee",       "2022",  "...",                        "4.5",                            "Not Counseled",
  "Data",      "...",               "...",   "...",                        "...",                            "...",
  "Data",      "Priya Patel",       "2022",  "11.0",                       "...",                            "Counseled",
  "...",       "...",               "...",   "...",                        "...",                            "...",
  "Preceptor", "Alice Becker",      "2026",  "8.0",                        "5.0",                            "Counseled",
  "Preceptor", "Brendan Cohen",     "2026",  "10.0",                       "10.0",                           "Not Counseled",
  "Preceptor", "...",               "...",   "...",                        "...",                            "...",
  "Preceptor", "Diego Ramirez",     "2026",  "12.5",                       "9.5",                            "Counseled",
  "...",       "...",               "...",   "...",                        "...",                            "..."
)

rcm_pop_primary_html <- gt::gt(rcm_pop_primary_tibble, id = "rcm_pop_primary") |>
  gt::tab_header(title = "Population Table --- Primary causal question") |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Unit/Time",          id = "unit_span",      columns = c(`Employee`, `Year`)) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Potential Outcomes", id = "outcome_span",   columns = c(`Contribution if Counseled`, `Contribution if Not Counseled`)) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Treatment",          id = "treatment_span", columns = c(`Counseling Status`)) |>
  gt::cols_align(align = "left",  columns = c(`Source`, `Employee`, `Counseling Status`)) |>
  gt::cols_align(align = "right", columns = c(`Year`, `Contribution if Counseled`, `Contribution if Not Counseled`)) |>
  gt::fmt_markdown(columns = gt::everything()) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    heading.title.font.size   = "1.5em",
    heading.title.font.weight = "bolder",
    column_labels.font.weight = "normal"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners()) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(align = "left"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = c("unit_span", "treatment_span"))) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(align = "right"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = "outcome_span")) |>
  gt::text_transform(
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = `Contribution if Not Counseled`, rows = c(8, 11)),
    fn = function(x) hatch(x)
  ) |>
  gt::text_transform(
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = `Contribution if Counseled`, rows = 9),
    fn = function(x) hatch(x)
  ) |>
  gt::tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Data rows are 2022 employees at the comparable tech company; Preceptor rows are our company's 2026 employees. Both blocks are samples from the same broader population of technology-sector employees in the 2022--2026 window.",
    locations = gt::cells_title()
  ) |>
  gt::opt_css(css = "#rcm_pop_primary .gt_footnote { max-width: 1px; word-break: break-word; }") |>
  gt::as_raw_html()

cat("```{=html}\n",
    '<div style="display: inline-block; width: auto; max-width: 100%;">',
    rcm_pop_primary_html, "</div>\n```\n", sep = "")
```

The structural shift from Preceptor Table to Population Table is the addition of the `Source` and `Year` columns and the use of `...` to mark separator rows (in the population at large, for which we have nothing). All four assumptions act on this table.

#### Validity

> *Validity is the consistency, or lack thereof, in the columns of the data set and the corresponding columns in the Preceptor Table.*

Validity is about the *columns*. Two columns can have the same name and still measure different things. For our setting, the most consequential validity worry is the `Counseling Status` column. We have called it the same thing in both the data and the Preceptor Table, but the underlying behavior at the comparison company may differ from the underlying behavior at our company. Maybe their "Counseling" condition consisted of a single 30-minute introductory session, while our planned program will be three sessions over six months. Same name, different treatment.

A second worry is the `Contribution Rate` column. The two companies' 401(k) plans may default new employees in at different starting rates. If the comparison company defaults at 5% and our company at 3%, then the same employee --- identical financial preferences, identical reactions to counseling --- would show different `Contribution Rate` values across the two settings, even with no behavioral difference. The column called `Contribution Rate` therefore does not measure the same underlying construct in both blocks.

#### Stability

> *Stability means that the relationship between the columns in the Population Table is the same for three categories of rows: the data, the Preceptor Table, and the larger population from which both are drawn.*

Stability is best understood as a statement about the *parameters* of the model, not about distributions. A change in the *level* of contribution rates between 2022 and 2026 (because of, say, broader wage growth or shifts in the share of mid-career employees) is a distribution shift; that on its own does not violate stability. A change in the *slope* connecting counseling status to contribution rate is a parameter shift; that is what violates stability.

For our setting, the broader macroeconomic environment differs significantly between 2022 and 2026. Inflation, interest rates, equity-market returns, and federal tax policy on retirement accounts have all shifted. The slope connecting financial counseling to contribution behavior likely depends on the broader environment: an employee in 2022 with low inflation and high market returns may respond very differently to a counseling session from an employee in 2026 facing different conditions. The parameter governing the counseling effect may therefore have shifted between the two eras, even if the marginal contribution rate has not changed much.

#### Representativeness

> *Representativeness, or the lack thereof, concerns two relationships among the rows in the Population Table. The first is between the data and the other rows. The second is between the other rows and the Preceptor Table.*

Representativeness is about the *rows*. Two links to defend. First, are the 2022 employees at the comparison company a random sample from the broader population of technology-company employees? Probably not. The comparison company may skew younger, more highly educated, more concentrated in a particular geography, or differently compensated than the technology-sector average. Second, are our 2026 employees a random sample from that same broader population? Again, probably not, and probably for different reasons. The first link fails if the comparison-company employees are systematically different from the broader population; the second link fails if our company's employees are systematically different. Both links have to hold for the model fit on 2022 data to transport cleanly to our 2026 setting.

When representativeness is violated, our parameter estimates *might* be biased --- not *will be*, because by chance the bias might wash out, but we have no principled reason to expect that to happen.

#### Unconfoundedness

> *Unconfoundedness means that the treatment assignment is independent of the potential outcomes, when we condition on pre-treatment covariates.*

Unconfoundedness applies only to causal models. The phrase *pre-treatment covariates* is load-bearing: conditioning on a covariate that sits on the causal path from treatment to outcome (controlling for occupation when estimating education's effect on income, say) introduces bias rather than removing it.

The cleanest defense of unconfoundedness is random assignment of treatment. If the comparison company had randomly assigned individual employees to either attend financial counseling or sit in a study-hall control, unconfoundedness would hold by construction within their data: treatment assignment would be independent of any unit-level characteristic, including the potential outcomes.

In practice, voluntary financial-counseling programs are voluntary --- there is no randomization. Employees who sign up differ from those who don't. They may be more financially literate, more risk-averse, more conscientious, more concerned about retirement, or going through a life event (marriage, birth, parent illness) that prompts the financial-planning conversation in the first place. All of these characteristics also affect 401(k) contribution rates through paths that have nothing to do with the counseling itself. So treatment assignment (signing up for counseling) is correlated with potential outcomes (the contribution rates the employee would set in either arm), and unconfoundedness fails. This is the classic *self-selection* problem in observational causal inference, and it is the chronic methodological challenge in voluntary financial-wellness programs.

> *There are known knowns. There are things we know we know. We also know there are known unknowns. That is to say, we know there are some things we do not know. But there are also unknown unknowns, the ones we do not know we do not know.* --- Donald Rumsfeld

The known unknowns in our setting are the covariates we wish we had: prior contribution rate, salary, tenure, marital status, dependents, financial-literacy score. If we observed all of them and conditioned on them, *selection on observables* might rescue unconfoundedness. The unknown unknowns are the covariates we cannot even name: the employee's underlying degree of risk aversion, their internal time preference, the specific life event that prompted them to sign up. *Selection on unobservables* is the harder case --- the case that requires research designs (instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, difference-in-differences) the example tutorials in later chapters will introduce.

#### The Population Table for the paired predictive question

The paired predictive question's Population Table has the same row structure but a single outcome column and no treatment column. The Data block is still the 2022 employees at the comparable tech company; the Preceptor block is our new hires expected in 2027 (the year after the counseling program would have launched).

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
rcm_pop_paired_tibble <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Source,     ~`Employee`,         ~`Year`, ~`Contribution Rate (%)`,
  "...",       "...",               "...",   "...",
  "Data",      "Sarah Kim",         "2022",  "9.0",
  "Data",      "Michael Lee",       "2022",  "4.5",
  "Data",      "...",               "...",   "...",
  "Data",      "Priya Patel",       "2022",  "11.0",
  "...",       "...",               "...",   "...",
  "Preceptor", "Erica Foster",      "2027",  "6.0",
  "Preceptor", "Frank Gutierrez",   "2027",  "10.0",
  "Preceptor", "...",               "...",   "...",
  "Preceptor", "Hannah Park",       "2027",  "4.5",
  "...",       "...",               "...",   "..."
)

rcm_pop_paired_html <- gt::gt(rcm_pop_paired_tibble, id = "rcm_pop_paired") |>
  gt::tab_header(title = "Population Table --- Paired predictive question") |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Unit/Time", id = "unit_span",    columns = c(`Employee`, `Year`)) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Outcome",   id = "outcome_span", columns = c(`Contribution Rate (%)`)) |>
  gt::cols_align(align = "left",  columns = c(`Source`, `Employee`)) |>
  gt::cols_align(align = "right", columns = c(`Year`, `Contribution Rate (%)`)) |>
  gt::fmt_markdown(columns = gt::everything()) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    heading.title.font.size   = "1.5em",
    heading.title.font.weight = "bolder",
    column_labels.font.weight = "normal"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners()) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(align = "left"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = "unit_span")) |>
  gt::tab_style(style = gt::cell_text(align = "right"),
                locations = gt::cells_column_spanners(spanners = "outcome_span")) |>
  gt::tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Data rows are 2022 employees at the comparable tech company; Preceptor rows are our company's expected 2027 new hires. No hatching --- predictive models have no potential outcomes.",
    locations = gt::cells_title()
  ) |>
  gt::opt_css(css = "#rcm_pop_paired .gt_footnote { max-width: 1px; word-break: break-word; }") |>
  gt::as_raw_html()

cat("```{=html}\n",
    '<div style="display: inline-block; width: auto; max-width: 100%;">',
    rcm_pop_paired_html, "</div>\n```\n", sep = "")
```

Validity, stability, and representativeness apply to this Population Table in the same ways they apply to the causal one above. Unconfoundedness does not; the paired question has no treatment to be confounded with anything.

#### Probability family and link function

Justice also commits us to the form of the model. The outcome `contribution_rate` is continuous (bounded below by 0 and above by 100, but in practice rarely near either extreme), so we will model it as Normal with the identity link:

$$Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$$

with $\mu = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_n X_n$. The example tutorials in later chapters will work through what to do when the outcome is *not* Normal --- binary, count, ordered --- but for our setting Normal is fine.

### Courage

> *Courage creates the data generating mechanism.*

Courage is brief. Justice gave us the form of the model and the assumptions we are willing to defend. Courage picks the specific covariates and *fits* the model.

What is a model? A model is a mathematical claim about how the data was generated. We write it as a formula that takes covariates as input and produces an expected outcome. The formula contains unknown numbers --- the *parameters* --- which we don't know in advance and have to estimate from the data. The act of fitting is the process of taking the data we have and finding the parameter values that best line up with it. After fitting, we have a concrete formula we can use to estimate outcomes for units we haven't seen, including the units in the Preceptor Table.

For our setting, the model would be a linear regression of contribution rate on treatment (and zero or more pre-treatment covariates). For the causal question, the fitted coefficient on the treatment variable is our estimate of the average causal effect. For the paired predictive question, the same coefficient is our estimate of the average difference in contribution rate between counseled and non-counseled employees. The math is the same; the interpretation depends on what assumptions we made in Justice.

### Temperance

> *Temperance interprets the data generating mechanism and then uses it to answer, with the help of graphics, the question(s) with which we began. Humility reminds us that this answer is always false.*

Temperance is where the fitted model becomes the answer. For the causal question, the answer is the average causal effect: a point estimate plus an interval. For the paired predictive question, the answer is the expected average contribution rate of next year's new hires, again with an interval. The interval is the model's measure of its own uncertainty --- a single number is half an answer.

Humility reminds us that the answer is never the truth. The posterior we report is, at best, the *Preceptor's Posterior* --- the best posterior achievable under our assumptions. The reported confidence interval captures only sampling variation under those assumptions; it does not capture our uncertainty about whether the assumptions themselves are correct. Validity is imperfect; stability is approximate; representativeness is stretched; unconfoundedness may fail. The reported interval is always too narrow.

## Summary

::: callout-tip
## Glossary

-   A **Preceptor Table** is the smallest possible table that includes all rows and columns such that, if no data is missing, it is easy to calculate our quantity of interest. Unfortunately, there is always data missing.

-   A **Population Table** has three sources of data: the Preceptor Table, the data set, and the greater population from which both are drawn.

-   A **Potential Outcome** is the outcome for an individual depending on if they receive the treatment or not.

-   A **Causal Effect** is the difference between two potential outcomes. The term **treatment effect** is used interchangeably with causal effect.

- **ATE**, the average treatment (or causal) effect, is the average of the causal (treatment) effect for the units of interest. We often drop the word "average" since it is implied. By convention, causal effect, treatment effect and average treatment effect all refer to the same (unknown) number.

-   The **Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference** is that it is impossible to observe the causal effect on a single unit because we can never observe both potential outcomes for that unit. We must make assumptions in order to estimate causal effects.

- When confronting a causal question, first identify the **units**, **treatments** and **outcomes**.

:::

The fundamental components of every problem in causal inference are units, treatments and outcomes. The units are the rows in the table. The treatments are (some of) the columns. The outcomes are the values under the treatment columns. (There are also covariate columns and the values within them.) **Whenever you confront a problem in causal inference, start by identifying the units, treatments and outcomes.**

A causal effect is the difference between one potential outcome and another. How different would your life be if you missed the train?

<!-- Replace Sliding Doors in Summary with something from Wonderful Life that references "An angel gets his wings."  Every time a predictive model is interpreted causally, a devil gets his pitchfork. -->

{{< video https://www.youtube.com/embed/BvUbv4iwbDs >}}

A Preceptor Table includes all rows and columns such that, if no data is missing, it is easy to calculate our quantity of interest. Unfortunately, data is always missing in causal models because, at most, we can only observe one *potential outcome* for each unit. The causal effect of a treatment on a single unit at a point in time is the difference between the value of the outcome variable with the treatment and without the treatment. The *Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference* is that it is impossible to observe the causal effect on a single unit. We must make assumptions --- i.e, we must make models --- in order to estimate causal effects.

The Population Table has three sources of data: the Preceptor Table, the data set, and the greater population from which both are drawn.

The assumption of *validity*, if met, allows us to create the Population Table because it ensures that the columns of data from the different sources can be put into a single table. If the relationships among the data are the same (or at least same'ish), over time, then we can assume *stability.* A model estimated on our data will also apply to our Preceptor Table. *Representativeness* examines the rows we have relative to the rows in the Population Table which we might have had. *Unconfoundedness*, which only matters in causal settings, means that either the treatment was randomly assigned or that we can act *as if* it was.

Random assignment of treatments to units is the best experimental set up for estimating causal effects. Other assignment mechanisms are subject to confounding. If the treatment assigned is correlated with the potential outcomes, it is very hard to estimate the true treatment effect. (As always, we use the terms "causal effects" and "treatment effects" interchangeably.) With random assignment, we can, mostly safely, estimate the average treatment effect (ATE) by looking at the difference between the average outcomes of the treated and control units.

Be wary of claims made in situations without random assignment: [Here be dragons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Here_be_dragons)!
